content:
# John Chapter 9 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JHN_009_page_001.jpg, JHN_009_page_002.jpg

1. And passing by, he saw a man blind from birth.

2. And the disciples of him asked him, saying, "Rabbi, who sinned, this [one] or the parents of him, that he was born blind?"

3. The Yeshua answered, "Neither this [one] sinned nor the parents of him, but [it was] that the works of the God might be revealed in him.

4. We must work the works of the [one] having sent me while it is day; night comes when no one is able to work.

5. When I am in the world, I am [the] light of the world."

6. Having said these things, he spat on the ground and made mud from the saliva, and he smeared the mud upon the eyes of him,

7. and said to him, "Go, wash in the pool of the Siloam" — which is translated "Sent." Then he went away and washed and came seeing.

8. Then the neighbors and the [ones] who had seen him before, that he was a beggar, said, "Is not this the [one] sitting and begging?"

9. Some said, "This is he." Others said, "No, but he is like him." That [one] said, "I am [he]."

10. Then they said to him, "How then were the eyes of you opened?"

11. That [one] answered, "The man called Yeshua made mud and smeared [it] on the eyes of me and said to me, 'Go to Siloam and wash.' Then having gone and having washed, I received sight."

12. And they said to him, "Where is that [one]?" He says, "I do not know."

13. They bring to the Pharisaioi the [one] once blind.

14. Now it was [the] Sabbath on the day when the Yeshua made the mud and opened the eyes of him.

15. Then again the Pharisaioi also asked him how he received sight. And he said to them, "He put mud on the eyes of me, and I washed, and I see."

16. Then some of the Pharisaioi said, "This man is not from the God, because he does not keep the Sabbath." But others said, "How is a sinful man able to do such signs?" And there was a division among them.

17. Then they say to the blind [one] again, "What do you say concerning him, because he opened the eyes of you?" And he said, "He is a prophet."

18. Then the Ioudaioi did not believe concerning him that he had been blind and received sight, until they called the parents of the [one] having received sight.

19. And they asked them, saying, "Is this the son of you, whom you say was born blind? How then does he now see?"

20. Then the parents of him answered and said, "We know that this is the son of us and that he was born blind.

21. But how he now sees we do not know, or who opened the eyes of him we do not know. Ask him — he is of age. He will speak concerning himself."

22. These things the parents of him said because they feared the Ioudaioi; for the Ioudaioi had already agreed that if anyone confessed him [as] Christos, he would be put out of the synagogue.

23. Because of this the parents of him said, "He is of age; ask him."

24. Then they called a second time the man who had been blind and said to him, "Give glory to the God! We know that this man is a sinner."

25. Then that [one] answered, "Whether he is a sinner I do not know. One thing I know: that being blind, now I see."

26. Then they said to him, "What did he do to you? How did he open the eyes of you?"

27. He answered them, "I told you already, and you did not hear. Why do you wish to hear again? Do you also wish to become disciples of him?"

28. And they reviled him and said, "You are a disciple of that [one], but we are disciples of Mouses.

29. We know that the God has spoken to Mouses; but this [one] — we do not know from where he is."

30. The man answered and said to them, "For in this is the marvel, that you do not know from where he is, and he opened the eyes of me.

31. We know that the God does not hear sinners, but if anyone is God-fearing and does the will of him, this [one] he hears.

32. From the age it was not heard that anyone opened [the] eyes of [one] born blind.

33. If this [one] were not from the God, he would not be able to do anything."

34. They answered and said to him, "In sins you were born entirely, and you teach us?" And they cast him out.

35. The Yeshua heard that they cast him out, and having found him, he said, "Do you believe in the Son of the Man?"

36. That [one] answered and said, "And who is he, Lord, that I may believe in him?"

37. The Yeshua said to him, "You have both seen him, and the [one] speaking with you is he."

38. And he said, "I believe, Lord." And he worshiped him.

39. And the Yeshua said, "For judgment I came into this world, that the [ones] not seeing may see, and the [ones] seeing may become blind."

40. [Some] of the Pharisaioi who were with him heard these things and said to him, "Are we also blind?"

41. The Yeshua said to them, "If you were blind, you would not have sin; but now you say, 'We see' — the sin of you remains."

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.4 — "We must work":** Sinaiticus reads ἡμᾶς ("we") rather than ἐμέ ("me/I"). Jesus includes his disciples in the mission — "we must work" rather than "I must work." This is the harder reading and likely original.

2. **v.7 — "Siloam, which is translated Sent":** John provides the etymology — Σιλωάμ means "Sent" (from the Hebrew שׁלח, shalach). The man born blind is sent to the pool of "Sent" by the one who is himself "the Sent One" of God. John's wordplay is deliberate.

3. **v.22 — ἀποσυνάγωγος ("put out of the synagogue"):** A formal excommunication. This term appears only in John (9:22, 12:42, 16:2). Some scholars argue it reflects the post-70 CE Birkat haMinim (the "blessing" against heretics added to synagogue prayers), suggesting John's community experienced such expulsion.

4. **v.35 — "Son of the Man":** Sinaiticus reads τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ("the Son of Man"), not "Son of God" as in some later manuscripts. The harder reading — "Son of Man" is more Johannine in this context, since the healed man does not yet know who Jesus is (v.36).

5. **v.38 — "He worshiped him":** προσεκύνησεν αὐτῷ — the same word used for worship of God. In context, this is the climactic act of the narrative: from "the man called Jesus" (v.11) to "a prophet" (v.17) to "from God" (v.33) to full worship.

— EMET, AI Translator
